s: George IV's seaside fantasy: minarets and onion domes by the English Channel
w: George, Prince of Wales, first took a farmhouse on this site in 1787. By the time John Nash finished the transformation in 1823 it was an Indo-Saracenic fever dream — Mughal domes, chhatri minarets and cast-iron columns clad as palm trees inside. William IV liked it; Victoria did not, and sold it to Brighton in 1850. During the First World War it served as a hospital for Indian soldiers, closing a circle the architecture had long hinted at.
_facts: Built in stages 1787–1823 as a seaside retreat for George, Prince of Wales (later George IV)
Current Indo-Saracenic exterior designed by John Nash, who remodelled it from 1815
Grade I listed former royal residence
Used as a military hospital for Indian soldiers in World War I
Sold to Brighton corporation by Queen Victoria in 1850

s: Where ABBA won Eurovision in 1974 — still in the same room
w: Built as the Prince Regent's stables and riding house in 1804, the domed rotunda once sheltered sixty horses in oriental splendour. On 6 April 1974 the same room hosted the Eurovision Song Contest: ABBA walked out and sang 'Waterloo', and pop music changed. The Corn Exchange next door was the grain market; the tunnel to the Royal Pavilion is still there. Grade I listed, all three venues still in use.
_facts: Originally built as the royal stables and riding house in 1804 for the Prince of Wales
Eurovision Song Contest 1974 was held here on 6 April 1974, where ABBA won with 'Waterloo'
Grade I listed building, part of the Royal Pavilion Estate
Linked to the Royal Pavilion by an underground tunnel

s: Britain's most beautiful ruin — Grade I listed and dissolving into the sea
w: Designed by Eugenius Birch and opened in 1866, this was the first pier in England to receive Grade I listing. Closed to the public in 1975, refused consent for demolition, hit by storms and arson, it now exists only as a skeletal lattice of oxidised ironwork. Starlings roost in their thousands here at dusk, murmurating around the ghost of its former concert hall. Nothing else quite looks like it.
_facts: Designed by Eugenius Birch, opened in 1866
First pier in England to be Grade I listed
Closed to the public in 1975 after storm damage
Damaged further by storms and two fires in 2003
Famous for its dramatic starling murmurations at dusk

s: The world's oldest operational electric railway, running since 1883
w: Magnus Volk switched on the first section of his beachfront railway in August 1883 — making this the oldest electric railway still running in the world. It predated Berlin's famous Gross-Lichterfelde tram by just two years and has outlasted everything. The narrow-gauge trains still trundle the same mile-and-a-quarter of seafront between Aquarium and Black Rock. Volk also built a stranger machine: a seaborne tram on stilts that waded through the sea to Rottingdean.
_facts: First section opened August 1883 by Magnus Volk
Oldest operational electric railway in the world
Runs narrow-gauge along Brighton seafront between Aquarium and Black Rock
Volk later built the 'Daddy Long Legs' seashore electric tram (1896-1901) as an extension

s: A glass doughnut sliding up a steel needle above the ghost of the West Pier
w: Designed by Marks Barfield — who gave the world the London Eye — the i360 rises from the landward end of the ruined West Pier's causeway. The pod lifts 150 metres above the promenade, putting the Downs, the Channel and Brighton's roofscape into one slow revolution. The West Pier Trust owns the base; the viewing pod is the main income funding its restoration.
_facts: Opened 4 August 2016, 162 metres tall
Designed by Marks Barfield, who also designed the London Eye
Stands at the landward end of the West Pier remains
Pod rises to 150 metres for 360-degree views
Proceeds help fund West Pier restoration

s: Britain's oldest cinema still running — films here since 1910
w: Opened on 22 September 1910 and never closed, this Edwardian picture house at Preston Circus is the strongest claimant to the title of Britain's oldest cinema in continuous operation. Cinema historian Allen Eyles called it 'deserving to be named Britain's oldest cinema.' It leans into its heritage without becoming a museum: comfy seats replaced the wooden benches, but the original decorative plasterwork survives, and the programming still skews independent and eccentric.
_facts: Opened 22 September 1910
Widely considered the oldest cinema in continuous use in Britain
Art house cinema programming
Cinema historian Allen Eyles endorsed its claim as Britain's oldest

s: The forgotten circus palace on Middle Street, built for water spectaculars
w: Frank Matcham designed this red-brick extravaganza for a circus impresario in 1897; it could flood its arena for aquatic shows. Later a music hall, variety theatre and bingo hall, it was also a television studio where famous names recorded. Closed in 2007 and Grade II* listed, the ornate interior — gilded boxes, baroque plasterwork — sits sealed inside the street-level facade, awaiting restoration that has been promised for two decades.
_facts: Designed by Frank Matcham and built in 1897
Originally built for circus and aquatic spectaculars with a floodable arena
Became a music hall, then TV studio, then bingo hall
Closed 2007; Grade II* listed building
Ornate interior survives but building has been closed and awaiting restoration

s: The tallest Victorian nave in England, a red-brick cliff near the station
w: Edmund Scott built this in 1872–74 for the working-class parish between the station and London Road, funding it largely on donations. The nave rises 44 metres — taller than Westminster Abbey's central tower. Outside: severe red brick, no spire. Inside: vertiginous scale, sparse and solemn, silver altar pieces by Henry Wilson. A great orphaned monument.
_facts: Built 1872-74, designed by architect Edmund Scott
Nave stands 44 metres high, the tallest Victorian church nave in England, taller than Westminster Abbey's central tower
Red brick exterior, Grade I listed
Interior contains silver altar pieces by Henry Wilson
Funded primarily by donations for a working-class parish

s: Ten million bricks on a sharp curve, carrying trains since the 1840s
w: John Urpeth Rastrick engineered this curving brick monster in the 1840s for the Brighton, Lewes and Hastings Railway. Twenty-seven arches, approximately ten million bricks, sharply curved — it should not work, but it does, and has since it was built. Grade II* listed, it now carries the East Coastway Line and is best seen from the streets below, where the brick canyon it creates runs through rows of Victorian terraces as though they grew up around it.
_facts: Built in the 1840s for the Brighton, Lewes and Hastings Railway by John Urpeth Rastrick
27 arches and approximately 10 million bricks
Sharply curving structure, Grade II* listed
Still in continuous use carrying the East Coastway Line

s: The world's oldest operating aquarium, open since 1872
w: Opened in 1872, the Brighton Aquarium was the largest in the world at the time, and the main tank still holds that original Victorian ambition. It was also home to a dolphinarium from 1969 to 1991. Now branded as Sea Life, it remains the oldest continuously operating aquarium anywhere on earth, and its subterranean Gothic vaults and horseshoe arches have outlasted every trend that passed through them.
_facts: Opened 1872 as Brighton Aquarium — the oldest continuously operating aquarium in the world
Main tank was the largest in the world at the time of opening
Operated as a dolphinarium from 1969 to 1991
Acquired by SEA LIFE in 1991
Victorian Gothic vaulted architecture survives throughout

s: Ten thousand toys in the Victorian arches under Brighton station
w: Four of the early Victorian brick railway arches that hold up the station forecourt have been filled, since 1991, with over ten thousand toys and models — tin plate trains, lead soldiers, cast-iron money banks, mid-century Dinky and Corgi sets, puppets, dolls. The arches are damp and slightly subterranean, which only adds to the atmosphere. Gauged railway layouts run throughout the low spaces, smoke optional.
_facts: Founded 1991 in Victorian railway arches under Brighton station forecourt
Holds over 10,000 toys and models
Occupies 4,000 square feet in four early Victorian arches
Working model railway layouts throughout the space

s: A 5,500-year-old Neolithic enclosure hidden inside a housing estate
w: Roughly a kilometre from the sea, on the hill above Brighton Racecourse, four concentric ditched circuits mark a Neolithic causewayed enclosure from around 3700 BC. This may have been a settlement, a ritual site, or a place for seasonal gathering — no one knows. The bones of a young woman and child were found in the ditches. Most of it is now a housing estate and a racecourse car park, which makes the grass-covered humps that survive feel stranger, not less so.
_facts: Causewayed enclosure built c.3700 BC, among the oldest known sites in Sussex
Four concentric ditch circuits still visible on Whitehawk Hill
Human remains found in the ditches during excavations
Most of the site now overlain by housing and racecourse
Scheduled Ancient Monument

s: Brighton's Victorian synagogue, serving a congregation formed in the 1820s
w: The Brighton Hebrew Congregation formed around the 1820s as Regency society made the town fashionable for all. The present building on Middle Street dates from 1875, in a Moorish-Italianate style by Thomas Lainson that sits oddly and wonderfully among the flint-and-stucco buildings of the town centre. Still an active Orthodox congregation, it is one of the few purpose-built Victorian synagogues in England still in regular use.
_facts: Congregation formed c.1820s as Brighton Hebrew Congregation
Current building dates from 1875, designed by Thomas Lainson
Moorish-Italianate architecture
Active Orthodox congregation, now known as Brighton & Hove Hebrew Congregation
Among the oldest purpose-built synagogues in England still in regular use

s: A 1722 Palladian mansion by a French architect, marooned in its own park
w: Nicholas Dubois — French-born architect and engineering officer — built this for the Pelham family in 1722 in a Palladian style that reads as an import, uncommon in Sussex. The house sits at the centre of Stanmer Park, one of Brighton's few surviving country estates with its own village church and estate cottages still intact. The Pelhams held it for over two centuries; the city council bought it in 1947. Now a restaurant and events venue, it remains Grade I listed.
_facts: Built 1722 by French architect Nicholas Dubois for the Pelham family
Palladian style, Grade I listed
Set in Stanmer Park, which includes a historic village and estate cottages
Owned by Brighton city council since 1947

s: A Norman hilltop church a thousand years old, surrounded by suburban Brighton
w: A place of Christian worship has occupied this chalk hill since at least the 12th century. The present Grade II* listed building preserves Norman internal features and a 13th-century exterior; inside, medieval wall paintings survive alongside memorials to Richard Shelley, once the most powerful nobleman in early Brighton's story. The churchyard looks down over the suburb that swallowed the village in the 20th century, as if the hill itself refuses to be absorbed.
_facts: Norman church with features dating to the 12th century and 13th-century exterior
Grade II* listed, over 1,000 years of Christian worship on site
Medieval wall paintings survive inside
Memorial to Richard Shelley, one of early Brighton's most important noblemen
Parish church of Patcham, an ancient village now absorbed into Brighton

s: In 1984 the IRA planted a bomb here to kill the British cabinet
w: John Whichcord Jr. built this French Second Empire block in 1864 as a hotel for the upper classes. Its place in history came at 2:54 am on 12 October 1984, when a bomb planted behind the bath panel of room 629 exploded, bringing down the hotel facade. The target was Margaret Thatcher and the Conservative cabinet assembled for the party conference. Five people died; Thatcher survived. The hotel was rebuilt and still operates. A plaque marks the room.
_facts: Built 1864, designed by John Whichcord Jr.
On 12 October 1984 the IRA detonated a bomb here targeting Margaret Thatcher and the Conservative cabinet
Five people killed, over thirty injured; Thatcher survived unhurt
The explosion destroyed the front facade of the hotel
Hotel was rebuilt and remains open

s: A Grade II* smock mill stranded in 1930s suburbia, still with all its machinery
w: Built around 1820 as a working corn mill, this hexagonal smock mill worked until 1897 and has been restored to show its original machinery intact. It sits on a small hill above Holmes Avenue, completely surrounded by inter-war semis that pressed up around it as Hove expanded outward — the mill predates its entire suburb by a century. Grade II* listed, open in summer seasons.
_facts: Built c.1820 as a working smock corn mill
Ground corn until 1897
Grade II* listed, hexagonal smock mill
Restored with original machinery intact and open to visitors

s: A cathedral-sized antiques emporium spilling across Kensington Gardens
w: Two floors of Kensington Gardens filled with over two hundred dealers' stalls, arranged in a perpetual organised chaos of mid-century furniture, vintage clothing, military ephemera, records, ceramics and objects with no obvious category. It has operated in North Laine since the 1970s and is the kind of place that looks smaller on the outside than in. Nothing is catalogued; everything has a price. An afternoon in here is reliable evidence that accumulation is a form of memoir.
_facts: Long-running antiques and vintage emporium in North Laine since the 1970s
Over 200 dealers' stalls across two floors
Located at 7-8 Kensington Gardens in the heart of North Laine
Specialises in mid-century furniture, vintage clothing, records and collectables

s: An Edwardian house left exactly as the Stanford family kept it, ghost included
w: The medieval manor of Preston was rebuilt in 1738 and again enlarged in 1905 to its current Edwardian form. When the last of the Stanford family — reputedly the richest family in Sussex — gave it to Brighton in 1932, they left the contents intact: the servants' quarters below stairs, the drawing rooms above, the silverware, the family photographs. The ghost of 'the White Lady' is said to haunt the upper floors. It operates as a historic house museum of Edwardian life.
_facts: Original structure dates to the 13th century, rebuilt 1738, enlarged 1905
Passed through the Stanford family, described as the richest family in Sussex
Given to Brighton corporation in 1932 with contents intact
Operates as an Edwardian house museum; Grade II* listed

s: North Laine record shop with a cult following well beyond Brighton
w: Since opening on Kensington Gardens in 2004, Resident has become one of the UK's most admired independent record shops — regularly on national best-of lists, known for knowledgeable staff and eclectic stock weighted toward independent labels. The in-store sessions and Record Store Day queue have made it a fixture of Brighton's musical identity. New and second-hand, every genre.
_facts: Independent record shop founded 2004 on Kensington Gardens, North Laine
Regularly listed among the UK's best independent record shops
Known for knowledgeable staff and curated eclectic stock
Regular in-store sessions and notable Record Store Day events

s: Charles Barry's pre-Westminster masterpiece: the unofficial cathedral of Brighton
w: Sir Charles Barry designed St Peter's before the Palace of Westminster made him famous — it shows the early mastery that got him the commission. Built 1824–28 on a traffic island between the A23 and A270, perpendicular Gothic pinnacles rise above the carriageways. It served as Brighton's parish church 1873–2007, quietly filling the cathedral role the town never had. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Designed by Sir Charles Barry, built 1824-28
Barry went on to design the Palace of Westminster
Considered the finest example of pre-Victorian Gothic Revival style in England
Grade II* listed; served as Brighton's parish church 1873-2007
Informally known as 'Brighton's cathedral'

s: One of England's highest racecourses, racing on Whitehawk Hill since the 1780s
w: Racing has taken place on Whitehawk Hill since the 1780s, at an altitude that makes this one of the highest flat-racing tracks in England. The horseshoe-shaped left-handed circuit is steeply undulating — unusual for flat racing — and the views from the grandstand extend across the city to the Channel. The course is famous for difficult-to-predict results, attributed by racing folk to the local sea breezes. Nearby, below the grandstand, Whitehawk Neolithic Camp predates the racing by five thousand years.
_facts: Racing on Whitehawk Hill since the 1780s
One of the highest flat racecourses in England
Unusual undulating circuit for flat racing
Left-handed horseshoe course with views of Brighton and the Channel
